core_story.problem: It has been observed that different pansharpening models tend to exhibit particular preferences towards different types of scenes and frequently encounter difficulties when applied to novel scenes [13, 28, 43], indicating a scene-dependency.
core_story.limitation: However, most of them are sensor-dependent, indicating that each satellite dataset requires a model tailored to the specific characteristics of sensors.
core_story.insight: At the same time, it has been observed that these models also exhibit scene dependence, for example, if the majority of the training samples come from the urban scenes, the model’s performance may decline in the river scene.
core_story.proposed_method: To address the domain gap produced by varying satellite sensors and dis- tinct scenes, we propose a dual-granularity semantic guided sparse routing diffusion model for general pansharpening.
core_story.experimental_evidence: Extensive experi- ments on WorldView-3, QuickBird, and GaoFen-2 datasets show the effectiveness of our proposed method.
core_story.summary: It has been observed that different pansharpening models tend to exhibit particular preferences towards different types of scenes and frequently encounter difficulties when applied to novel scenes [13, 28, 43], indicating a scene-dependency. However, most of them are sensor-dependent, indicating that each satellite dataset requires a model tailored to the specific characteristics of sensors. At the same time, it has been observed that these models also exhibit scene dependence, for example, if the majority of the training samples come from the urban scenes, the model’s performance may decline in the river scene. To address the domain gap produced by varying satellite sensors and dis- tinct scenes, we propose a dual-granularity semantic guided sparse routing diffusion model for general pansharpening. Extensive experi- ments on WorldView-3, QuickBird, and GaoFen-2 datasets show the effectiveness of our proposed method.

1. Introduction
Due to the technological and physical limitations of imaging
devices, remote sensing images obtained by satellite sensors
always have a trade-off between spatial and spectral resolu-
tion [47]. Low-Resolution MultiSpectral (LRMS) images,
which consist of multiple spectral bands, have relatively
low spatial resolution. In contrast, single-band panchromatic
(PAN) images exhibit high spatial resolution. Therefore,
pansharpening is a fundamental and essential process to
fuse an LRMS image with a PAN image to produce a High-

limitation_expression: However, most of them are sensor-dependent, indicating that each satellite dataset requires a model tailored to the specific characteristics of sensors.

method_expression: To address the domain gap produced by varying satellite sensors and dis- tinct scenes, we propose a dual-granularity semantic guided sparse routing diffusion model for general pansharpening.
In this paper, we propose a Semantic-Guided Sparse Rout- ing Diffusion Model (SGDiff) for general pansharpening.
Furthermore, our method demonstrates excellent zero-shot capability, proving the robustness of SGDiff in real-world scenarios.

The main contributions are summarized as follows. • We propose a Semantic-Guided Sparse Routing Diffu- sion Model (SGDiff) that leverages large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to narrow the domain gap among various satellite sensors and scenes for general pansharpening. • The Semantic-Guided Mixture of Expert (SG-MoE) block is designed to dynamically activate specific experts by integrating semantic priors derived from both scene-level and region-level guidance, thereby adapting to varying input characteristics. • The results demonstrate that SGDiff achieves state-of-the- art performance under reduced and full resolution settings, with superior performance in zero-shot generalization.
experiment_expression: Nevertheless, dataset-level guidance alone in above methods is insufficient to adequately capture the diversity of satellite imagery, since significant variations exist in scenes and ground objects even within the same satellite dataset, as shown in Figure 1.

The main contributions are summarized as follows. • We propose a Semantic-Guided Sparse Routing Diffu- sion Model (SGDiff) that leverages large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to narrow the domain gap among various satellite sensors and scenes for general pansharpening. • The Semantic-Guided Mixture of Expert (SG-MoE) block is designed to dynamically activate specific experts by integrating semantic priors derived from both scene-level and region-level guidance, thereby adapting to varying input characteristics. • The results demonstrate that SGDiff achieves state-of-the- art performance under reduced and full resolution settings, with superior performance in zero-shot generalization.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
Pansharpening aims at integrating complementary infor-
mation from panchromatic and multispectral images. Avail-
able deep-learning based pansharpening methods typically
perform exceptionally with particular satellite datasets. At
the same time, it has been observed that these models also
exhibit scene dependence, for example, if the majority of the
training samples come from the urban scenes, the model’s
performance may decline in the river scene. To address the
domain gap produced by varying satellite sensors and dis-
tinct scenes, we propose a dual-granularity semantic guided
sparse routing diffusion model for general pansharpening.
By utilizing the large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) in the
field of geoscience, e.g, GeoChat, we introduce the dual gran-
ularity semantics to generate dynamic sparse routing scores
for adaptation of different satellite sensors and scenes. This
scene-level and region-level dual-granularity semantic infor-
mation serves as guidance for dynamically activating spe-
cialized experts within the diffusion model. Extensive experi-
ments on WorldView-3, QuickBird, and GaoFen-2 datasets
show the effectiveness of our proposed method. Notably, the
proposed method outperforms the comparison approaches
in adapting to new satellite sensors and scenes. The codes
are available at https://github.com/codgodtao/SGDiff.
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
Due to the technological and physical limitations of imaging
devices, remote sensing images obtained by satellite sensors
always have a trade-off between spatial and spectral resolu-
tion [47]. Low-Resolution MultiSpectral (LRMS) images,
which consist of multiple spectral bands, have relatively
low spatial resolution. In contrast, single-band panchromatic
(PAN) images exhibit high spatial resolution. Therefore,
pansharpening is a fundamental and essential process to
fuse an LRMS image with a PAN image to produce a High-
*Equal contribution
†Corresponding authors
Resolution MultiSpectral (HRMS) image.
Traditional pansharpening methods rely on manually de-
signed transformations [ 8, 16], decompositions [ 1–3], or
optimization models [23, 30, 38], leading to suboptimal re-
sults in either enhancing spatial details or preserving spectral
information. In recent years, deep learning based methods
have gained significant attention by effectively learning the
relationships between HRMS and LRMS/PAN image pairs,
resulting in superior performance across various datasets.
However, most of them are sensor-dependent, indicating that
each satellite dataset requires a model tailored to the specific
characteristics of sensors. To address the sensor-dependent
issue, some general pansharpening methods have been pro-
posed [5, 35, 46, 50]. Wang et al. [46] combined the advan-
tages of deep learning and variational optimization to pro-
pose a universal pansharpening methods that can be applied
across different satellites. Sheng et al. [35] utilized a shared
spatial enhancement network to improve spatial details and
a branch of satellite-specific spectral adjustment networks
to capture the spectral characteristics. Xing et al. [50] intro-
duced the pioneering text-prompt 3D diffusion model, which
was jointly trained on various satellite datasets to acquire
generalized insights across various satellites. Nevertheless,
dataset-level guidance alone in above methods is insufficient
to adequately capture the diversity of satellite imagery, since
significant variations exist in scenes and ground objects even
within the same satellite dataset, as shown in Figure 1.
Satellite imagery exhibits distinct characteristics when
compared with natural images. Because satellite images are
captured from high vertical heights, they encompass a wide
range of ground object categories, such as urban environ-
ments, green vegetation, water bodies, and more [12, 28]. It
has been observed that different pansharpening models tend
to exhibit particular preferences towards different types of
scenes and frequently encounter difficulties when applied
to novel scenes [13, 28, 43], indicating a scene-dependency.
This phenomenon can be explained by the imbalanced allo-
cation of training data with substantial variations in scene
characteristics. For instance, if the majority of training exam-
ples are drawn from urban scenes, the model’s performance

bulldings {<40><55><56><63>|11>

scene. These buildings are likely part
of a city or urban area, and their
proximity suggests they may be used for
various purposes, such as residential,
commercial, or industrial activities ......
(c)
Figure 1. Motivation behind SGDiff, where (a) presents distinct scenes (Building and Agriculture) captured by the identical satellite (GF-2),
and (b) depicts the same scene observed from different satellites. Both scenarios can lead the model trained on a single satellite dataset
to overly rely on specific sensors and scenes. (c) Grounding description from GeoChat, which emphasizes object grounding and spatial
relationships, providing useful region-level a priori information for pansharpening.
will degrade in river scenes. A feasible solution is grouping
these training samples to build multiple models. However,
this is unrealistic due to the diversity of scene types. Al-
though different satellite sensors exhibit varying imaging
characteristics, they share similar surface attributes, referred
to as inter-sensor similarity, as illustrated in Figure 1 (b).
Consequently, jointly training multiple satellite datasets is
beneficial for addressing the scene-dependency challenge.
From another perspective, the strong generalization poten-
tial of remote sensing foundation models allows for the ex-
traction of certain scene-specific semantic priors, which can
further effectively exploit scene characteristics and enhance
the generalization ability of pansharpening [21, 36, 50]. In
this paper, we propose a Semantic-Guided Sparse Rout-
ing Diffusion Model (SGDiff) for general pansharpening.
Specifically, we prompt GeoChat [ 21], a vision-language
model customized for geoscience and refined using the re-
mote sensing multimodal instruction dataset, to extract the
dual-granularity semantics, including scene and grounding
descriptions. This semantic information serves as condi-
tions within the diffusion model, providing guidance at both
the scene and region levels, while dynamically activating
specialized experts [ 10, 14, 48]. We develop a Semantic-
Guided Mixture of Expert (SG-MoE) block to facilitate the
selection and scaling of channels tailored to each input. Fi-
nally, an effective denoising network is constructed for joint
training across multiple satellite datasets, acquiring gener-
alized knowledge among different datasets and distinguish-
ing between different scenes and ground objects. The pro-
posed SGDiff achieves superior performance on several satel-
lite datasets, especially at full resolution. Furthermore, our
method demonstrates excellent zero-shot capability, proving
the robustness of SGDiff in real-world scenarios. The main
contributions are summarized as follows.
• We propose a Semantic-Guided Sparse Routing Diffu-
sion Model (SGDiff) that leverages large Vision-Language
Models (VLMs) to narrow the domain gap among various
satellite sensors and scenes for general pansharpening.
• The Semantic-Guided Mixture of Expert (SG-MoE) block
is designed to dynamically activate specific experts by
integrating semantic priors derived from both scene-level
and region-level guidance, thereby adapting to varying
input characteristics.
• The results demonstrate that SGDiff achieves state-of-the-
art performance under reduced and full resolution settings,
with superior performance in zero-shot generalization.
raw_sections.conclusion:
5. Conclusion
In this paper, we introduce a dual-granularity Semantic-
Guided Sparse Routing Diffusion Model (SGDiff), which
leverages the capabilities of large Vision-Language Mod-
els (VLMs), specifically GeoChat, to address scene and
sensor dependency challenges in pansharpening. The dual-
granularity semantics derived from GeoChat are employed
to generate dynamic sparse routing weights, which are then
employed to dynamically activate specialized experts in the
designed Semantic-Guided Mixture of Expert (SG-MoE)
block. The proposed model demonstrates enhanced adapt-
ability and superior representation capacity. Extensive ex-
periments conducted across various satellite datasets reveal
SGDiff’s exceptional performance in both reduced and full-
resolution settings. It also demonstrates strong generalization
capabilities on unseen datasets, highlighting its practical ap-
plicability and suitability for real-world applications.
Acknowledgement
This work was supported in part by the National Natural
Science Foundation of China (NFSC) under Grant 62476223
and 62201467; in part by Innovation Capability Support
Program of Shaanxi (Program No. 2024ZC-KJXX-043), and
in part by the Young Talent Fund of Xi’an Association for
Science and Technology under Grant 959202313088.
